segments.s441-g65: به یک سال is a temporal/durational phrase meaning “for one year” (به + period). در سیستان is the locative “in Sistan.” سوک is the subject/noun “mourning,” and بود is the past-tense copula “was.” The order is somewhat poetic: modifiers (duration, place) precede the noun and the past verb; literally, “For a year in Sistan mourning was.”
segments.s441-g66: همه modifies جامه‌هاشان and means “all” or “every.” جامه‌هاشان is the plural noun جامه + plural marker ها + possessive suffix شان (“their garments”). سیاه و کبود are predicative adjectives following the noun (“black and dark-blue”); the copula is omitted here but is understood from the previous بود (i.e., their garments were black and dark-blue).
segments.s441-g9: چو is a temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. آگاه شد is a light verb construction meaning 'became aware' or 'learned' (آگاه = aware, شد = became); the verb phrase precedes the subject in this poetic word order. شاه کابلستان is the subject 'the king of Kabulistan' and follows the verb here as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s441-g10: ازان introduces a genitive/partitive sense 'of those' (pointing back to the men just mentioned). نامداران is a plural substantive 'the renowned ones' and زابلستان is the region specifying those men (Zabulistan) — the noun phrase means 'those famed men of Zabulistan.'
segments.s441-g11: سپاه پراگنده را is a definite/ topical object phrase 'the scattered host' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). گرد کرد is a compound verb meaning 'made [them] gather' / 'gathered' (گرد + کرد); گرد here is the verbal stem implying 'around/assemble' and کرد is the light verb 'to do/make'.
segments.s441-g12: This is a paired, elliptical poetic statement: زمین آهنین شد = 'the earth became iron'; then هوا لاژورد is parallel and understood as 'the air/sky (became) lapis-lazuli' although شد is not repeated. آهنین is an adjective 'iron/steely' and لاژورد is a poetic color 'lapis-lazuli/azure.'
segments.s441-g13: پذیره here denotes reception/host; followed by the proper name فرامرز and شد 'became' gives the sense 'Faramarz took them in / acted as host.' با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army/host' indicating accompaniment. Word order places the predicate before the agent (poetic style).
segments.s441-g14: بشد is a poetic past/resultative ('it became' / 'there came to be'); روشنایی is 'light/brightness' and ز introduces the source 'from' (poetic form). خورشید و ماه 'sun and moon' are the sources of that light. The clause means 'light arose from the sun and moon.'
segments.s441-g15: سپه را uses را to mark the definite/topic 'the host.' چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as.' روی اندر آمد is an idiomatic expression 'its face/appearance came in' = 'appeared'; به روی is a locative 'upon' that links forward to the following noun (جهان). The repetition of روی emphasizes 'appearance/coming into sight.'
segments.s441-g16: جهان شد 'the world became' is followed by stacked adjectival descriptors; پرآواز here means 'full of loud sound / resounding' (sonorous) and پرخاشجوی is 'belligerent/aggressive,' so the clause reads 'the world grew loud and quarrelsome.' This is a typical predicative sequence after شد.
segments.s441-g33: Verb-final classical order: بکشتند (they killed) is the main verb applying to the preceding quantitative phrase. چندان modifies the extent ('so many'); ز + گردان هند specifies the source/group killed ('from the Indian troop').
segments.s441-g34: هم ('also') links this clause to the previous; از بر here functions as a compound prepositional sense 'from among / out of' (بر fused with از), introducing the class targeted. منش is used in the sense 'of origin/lineage' modifying نامداران ('renowned ones'), and سند names the region (Sind). The verb is implied to continue from the previous clause ('they killed').
segments.s441-g35: که introduces the following result/explanation; گل شد means 'became mud' (گل = mud/clay here) describing خاک آوردگاه ('the soil/ground of the battlefield'). همی is a poetic intensifier/continuative adverb attached before خاک to stress the state.
segments.s441-g36: پراگنده شد is a passive/resultative clause 'was scattered / became dispersed'; هند و سندی سپاه is the subject 'the army/armies of Hind and of Sind' (سندی = 'of Sindh'). Word order places predicate before the subject, a common poetic structure.
segments.s441-g37: دل ... برداشتند is an idiomatic construction: برداشتند ('they removed/took away') governs دل ('heart') figuratively meaning 'they lost heart / they fled'. از مرز وز خانه should be read as از مرز و ز خانه ('from the border and from the house'); وز here is a contracted poetic form meaning 'and (from)'. The clause therefore says they abandoned frontier and home.
segments.s441-g38: This clause lists who was left behind: زن و کودک خرد — 'women and small children'. بگذاشتند is the verb 'they left (behind)'; here it means 'they left (them), abandoned them'. Note خرد is an adjective after کودک meaning 'small/young'.
segments.s441-g39: Noun phrase with modifiers: تن ('body') followed by مهتر کابلی ('the Kabuli chief/headman'). پر ز خون is a descriptive phrase 'full of / spattered with blood' where پر (full) takes ز + خون ('of blood'). Together this prepares the direct object for the following verb.
segments.s441-g40: فگنده is a past participle ('thrown') describing what was done to that body; it functions predicatively with the following prepositional complement به صندوق پیل اندرون ('into the elephant's chest/box inside'). The sequence shows the result: the bloodied body was cast into the elephant's howdah/box.
segments.s441-g49: چهل modifies خویش: a numeral + noun phrase 'forty kinsmen'. The sequence خوی + ش is the older enclitic possessive (his); او is the independent pronoun (here emphatic/identifying) and را is the definite-object marker, so the whole chunk means 'forty of his kinsmen' as the object. بر آتش نهاد is a simple past action: بر 'on/upon', آتش 'the fire', نهاد past of نهادن 'to put/lay' — 'placed on the fire'.
segments.s441-g50: ازان جایگه 'from that place' (ازان = 'from that', جایگه = 'place/position'). رفت is the past of رفتن 'went'. سوی + noun marks direction 'toward'; شغاد is the destination/name. The segment is a simple past-action clause attached to the prior clause by narrative sequence.
segments.s441-g51: به کردار 'in the manner' (به used adverbially + کردار 'manner/behavior'), کوه qualifies the manner 'like a mountain'. آتشی is an adjectival/noun form 'fiery / a fire', and برفروخت is a poetic past-formation meaning 'kindled/set ablaze' (بر- + فروخت used here intransitively/transitively as 'set on fire'). The whole clause says he kindled a fire with mountain-like force.
segments.s441-g52: This is a compound direct-object list (شغاد و چنار و زمین) followed by the object marker را and the past verb بسوخت 'burned'. The verb is active past; the list names the things/people consumed by the fire. The coordinating و joins items in a standard enumerative sequence.
segments.s441-g53: چو here = 'when' (temporal conjunction). لشکر is the subject 'the army'; سوی زابلستان marks the direction 'toward Zabulistan'. کشید is the past of کشیدن 'drew/moved' used here to mean 'marched/moved' (classical narrative past). The clause sets a temporal frame for what follows.
segments.s441-g54: همه خاک را is the object 'all the earth/soil' (همه 'entire', خاک 'earth', را object marker). سوی دستان کشید repeats the verb 'drew' with direction 'toward the hands' (دستان usually 'hands' but can be idiomatic). The verb کشید again expresses the action of drawing/moving the land; word order is subject-elided (understood from previous clause) + object + direction + verb.
segments.s441-g55: چو = 'when'. روز جفاپیشه is a genitival/attributive phrase 'day(s) of oppression/cruelty' (جفاپیشه is a compound meaning oppressive/cruel practicer). کوتاه کرد is a causative past 'made short/shortened' — i.e., the period of hardship was brought to an end. The clause means 'when the oppressive days were shortened'.
segments.s441-g56: به کابل = 'to/at Kabul' (prepositional phrase marking the place of the action). یکی مهتری = 'a certain elder/chief' (یکی 'a certain', مهتری 'preeminent one, chief/elder'). شاه کرد is the causative construction 'made (him) king' (کرد = 'made'). Together: 'he made a certain chief the king of Kabul.'
segments.s441-g57: ازان (poetic variant of از آن) introduces origin/possession: 'from that'. دودمان is the noun 'lineage/house'. کس here is used in a negative construction (with نماند) meaning 'no one'. به کابل gives the location 'in Kabul' (به used locatively). نماند is past negative-like 'was left / remained' — combined with کس it yields 'there remained no one'.
segments.s441-g58: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. منشور here means 'proclamation/charter' and تیغ is 'sword' forming a genitive phrase 'the sword's proclamation'. ورا is the clitic 'to him/it' (third person). برنخواند is the negative past form of خواندن with the verbal prefix بر- here meaning 'read aloud' — in context the clause means 'who did not read aloud (to him)'. Together with the previous segment the idiom 'کس ... نماند که ...' means 'there was no one who did not ...' (i.e. everyone did it).
segments.s441-g59: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'. کابل is the place. بیامد is simple past 'he came'. پر از is a fixed prepositional phrase 'full of' and is followed by a list of what fills him: داغ 'burning grief/mourning' and دود 'smoke'. The phrase describes his condition on arrival.
segments.s441-g60: شده is the past participle/compound meaning 'become' or 'was made', here introducing the resultant appearance. روز روشن is an adverbial phrase 'in the clear daylight' (روز = day, روشن = bright/clear). بروبر is an adverb 'completely/exactly' intensifying the adjective that follows. کبود is an adjective 'bruised/dusky' describing his appearance.
segments.s441-g61: خروشان is a participial/epithetic adjective 'roaring/tumultuous' describing the subject. همه زابلستان means 'all of Zabulistan' (the whole province). و بست uses بست (perfect/past) meaning 'bound/closed' or 'made fast' — here poetically implying they drew together or 'closed in' as a group.
segments.s441-g62: یکی را is an emphasized object/topic 'not one (person)'. نبد is a negative existential/past form 'there was not / did not exist' (i.e. 'not one had'). جامه 'garment' is the object, بر تن is the locative phrase 'upon the body' (بر = on/upon, تن = body). درست is an adjective 'proper/correct', so the clause states that there was not a single person properly clothed.
segments.s441-g63: به پیش is a locative phrase 'to the presence of / before'. فرامرز is the proper name of the addressee. باز آمدند is the pluperfect/simple past plural 'they returned/they came back' (باز = again, آمدند = they came), so the segment means 'they returned to Faramarz's presence'.
segments.s441-g64: دریده is a past-participial adjective 'torn' (often implying torn clothes or a torn heart). بر here functions as 'upon/on' (implying torn upon them—the missing noun like 'garments' is understood from context). و = 'and', با = 'with', گداز is a poetic noun 'ardor/anguish' (burning grief), and آمدند repeats the action 'they came'. Together: 'they came, torn and with anguish.'
segments.s441-g25: فرامرز is the proper name (subject/topic). با is the preposition 'with'. خوارمایه modifies سپاه and here describes the troops as contemptible/insignificant (an adjectival/epithet use); سپاه is the noun 'army/host'. The whole phrase is a prepositional phrase 'with a meager/lowly army' attached to the subject.
segments.s441-g26: بزد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb meaning 'struck/attacked' (here idiomatically 'hurled himself at'). خویشتن را is a reflexive object 'himself' with the object marker را (را marks the definite object/emphatic object). بر آن قلبگاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon that heart/core' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, قلبگاه = core/heart). In classical narrative خویشتن را بزد بر X = 'he hurled himself upon X'.
segments.s441-g27: ز introduces a source/cause ('from' or 'because of'). گرد here is the noun 'dust' (گردِ سواران = the dust kicked up by the horsemen) with the genitive سواران 'of the horsemen'. هوا is the subject 'the air/atmosphere'; تار شد is a predicative phrase 'became dim/obscured' (تار here is an older/poetic adjective meaning dark/obscure and شد marks change of state). The clause literally: 'From the horsemen's dust the air became dark.'
segments.s441-g28: سپهدار کابل is a noun phrase 'the commander of Kabul' (title + place). گرفتار شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'became trapped/was taken' — گرفتار used adjectivally ('caught, trapped') plus شد indicating the change/state. Together: 'the commander of Kabul was trapped.'
segments.s441-g29: پراگنده شد is a predicative construction: پراگنده 'scattered' used with شد 'became' to mean 'was scattered'. آن سپاه بزرگ is the noun phrase 'that great army/host' placed after the verb for narrative emphasis (verb then subject). بزرگ modifies سپاه as an adjective.
segments.s441-g30: دلیران زابل is a genitive phrase 'the brave ones of Zabul' (دلیران = brave men, زابل = Zabul). به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/behavior of' (به expressing manner, کردار = conduct/way). گرگ is the simile noun 'wolf'. Together: 'the brave men of Zabul ... in the manner of wolves' (i.e. scattered like wolves).
segments.s441-g31: ز هر سو means 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every direction/side). بریشان is a poetic contraction of بر + ایشان ('against them' / 'upon them') where بر carries the sense 'against/over' plus the third-person plural pronoun. کمین ساختند is the collocation 'they made/set ambushes' (کمین = ambush, ساختند = they made), with ساختند as the past plural verb.
segments.s441-g32: پس is a sequence particle 'then/after that'. لشکراندر here denotes the forces/troops (an archaic or compound form referring to the army/soldiers) used as the subject. همی is an archaic emphatic/continuative particle often found in epic narrative before a past-tense verb; it adds force or continuity. تاختند is the past plural 'they charged/attacked' (تاختن = to rush/attack).
segments.s441-g41: بیاورد is a 3rd-person past narrative verb meaning “brought” (verb before object in this archaic/epic word order). لشکر is the direct object “the army/host.” به introduces the destination (‘to’) and نخچیرگاه is a compound noun meaning “hunting-ground.”
segments.s441-g42: به جایی = “to a place.” کجا here is a relative/interrogative element used in an older syntax to mean “where” (introducing the relative clause). کنده بودند is the past construction: کنده (past participle) + بودند (auxiliary) = “they had dug/dug up.” چاه is the object ‘pit/well’ placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s441-g43: همی is a habitual/continuous narrative particle (often marking ongoing or emphatic action). برد here functions as a narrative past verb “took” or “carried off.” بدخواه را is the definite object marked by را (“the ill-wisher/enemy”). بسته دست is a verbless adjectival phrase ‘with hands bound’ (بسته = bound/tied modifying دست = hand), the adjective before the noun in this older poetic ordering.
segments.s441-g44: ز خویشان او = ‘of/from his relatives’ (ز = from/of). نیز = ‘also/likewise.’ چل is the numeral ‘forty’ placed before the noun, and بت‌پرست = ‘idolater(s).’ The phrase means that among his kin there were forty idolaters (or he likewise seized forty idolaters from his relatives).
segments.s441-g45: ز پشت سپهبد = ‘from the back of the spahbed (the general).’ زهی is an interjection/exclamation (׳zahī׳) meaning “ah!” or “how (wonderful/terrible)” rather than the noun ‘zahid’ here. برکشید is 3rd-person past “he lifted” or “he pulled up/drew out.” The prepositional phrase precedes the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s441-g46: چنان = ‘so/thus,’ linking result to the previous action. کاستخوان = ‘bone(s).’ و پی آمد پدید is a result clause: آمد = ‘came,’ پدید = ‘appeared/was revealed.’ Together it says that bone (and the flesh/sinew) came into view — i.e. the lifting exposed bone and inner tissue.
segments.s441-g47: ز چاه اندر = ‘into/from the well; in the well’ (poetic placement of prepositions). آویختنش is آویختن ‘to hang’ with the 3rd-person possessive/enclitic -ش = ‘him’ → ‘they hanged him’ or ‘his hanging.’ سرنگون is an adjectival/resultative participle meaning ‘overthrown/cast down/fell headlong.’ The clause states the action (hanging him into the well) and the resulting state (fallen/overthrown).
segments.s441-g48: تنش = تن (body) + enclitic -ش ‘his’ = ‘his body.’ پر ز خاک = ‘full of dust’ (پر = full (of) + ز = of/from + خاک = dust). و دهن پر ز خون = ‘and his mouth full of blood’ — same pattern repeated. This is a tightly compressed descriptive string typical of epic Persian: possesive noun + full-of phrases.
segments.s441-g1: چون here functions as a conjunction 'when / as' introducing a temporal clause. سوک 'mourning/dirge' is the direct object of the implied verb 'to raise' and بداشت is an archaic past form meaning 'raised/took up' (past, 3rd person singular) used poetically; the proper name فرامرز is the subject. The clause thus means 'When Farāmarz took up (raised) Rostam's dirge.'
segments.s441-g2: سپه را is the definite object (را marks the army as definite). همه modifies سپه ('the whole / entire army'). سوی هامون is a directional phrase 'toward the Hamun [plain]'. گذاشت is the past tense verb 'placed / sent / deployed' (3rd person singular). Together: 'he deployed/sent the entire army toward Hamun.'
segments.s441-g3: در خانه پیلتن is a locative phrase 'in the house of Piltan' (پیلتن is a place/name). باز here has the meaning 'open / opened' and together with کرد (past 3rd sing. of کردن) yields 'opened' or 'set open.' The subject is omitted (understood from previous clause).
segments.s441-g4: سپه را is again the definite object 'the army'. ز (poetic for از) introduces source 'from', so ز گنج پدر = 'from (his) father's treasure' (گنج پدر is a genitive/possessive grouping). ساز کردن is a light verbal phrase meaning 'to make ready / to equip'; with کرد (past) it yields 'he equipped (it).' Word order places the object before the source and the verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s441-g5: سحرگه is an adverbial 'at dawn'. خروش آمد is a compound where خروش 'a cry/clamor' is the noun and آمد (past of آمدن) means 'came / arose'—a common Persian pattern 'X آمد' = 'X arose' or 'there came X'. از کرنای specifies source 'from the trumpets' (کرنا/کرنای = trumpet or trumpet-blast).
segments.s441-g6: هم 'also/likewise' links additional sources of the sound. از introduces each source: کوس 'war-drum', و is the coordinating 'and', رویین 'iron/iron-made' describes another sounding source (iron clashing or iron instruments/armour), و again 'and', هندی 'Indian' modifies درای. درای here is an adjective/participle meaning 'possessing / wealthy' or more loosely 'of such make/possession', so the phrase lists further sources of the clamour: drums, iron (clashing), and Indian (instruments/wealth).
segments.s441-g7: سپاهی functions as a collective subject 'an army / troops'. ز زابل is the origin 'from Zabul' and به کابل is the destination 'to Kabul'. کشید is the past tense (3rd person singular) of کشیدن used here in the sense 'to march / to move (toward)'—a conventional epic/poetic usage meaning the army advanced.
segments.s441-g8: که introduces a result or consequential clause 'so that / and (thus)'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun' and گشت (past of گشتن) means 'became / seemed', here functioning as a copular/result verb. از جهان ناپدید is a prepositional phrase 'hidden/vanished from the world' with ناپدید as the predicative adjective. Together the clause expresses a hyperbolic result: 'the sun became vanished from the world.'
segments.s441-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source or cause; it introduces the noun phrase انبوه پیلان ('a multitude/host of elephants'). گرد سپاه is a genitival phrase 'the dust of the army' where گرد is a noun 'dust' and سپاه 'army' is its possessor; the coordinating و simply links the two noun phrases.
segments.s441-g18: به بیشه درون is a locative phrase 'into/within the thicket' (به + noun + درون 'within'). شیر is the subject 'the lion'. گم گرد is a compacted poetic verbal phrase meaning 'became lost' (an archaic/poetic variant of گم گشت/گم شد), and راه is the object 'the way' (the lion became lost as to the road). Word order places the locative before the subject and verbal phrase, typical in epic diction.
segments.s441-g19: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' initiating the clause. یکی باد is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/a single wind' (یکی used as an indefinite article). The coordinating و links گردی کبود, where گردی is a noun 'a dust-cloud' and کبود is an adjective 'dusky/bluish' following the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s441-g20: This clause is negative existential: زمین (the earth/ground) with the prepositional phrase ز آسمان ('from the sky' or 'as seen from the sky') is followed by هیچ پیدا نبود, literally 'was not visible at all.' هیچ negates پیدا نبود ('was not visible/was not to be seen') — نبود is past 'was not.'
segments.s441-g21: بیامد is a past verb 'came' placed before the proper name (verb-first order is common in narrative). پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the army' (پیش meaning 'before/in front of').
segments.s441-g22: دو دیده نبرداشت is an idiomatic negative perfect/past construction: نبرداشت (negative of برداشتن) here means 'did not remove' — i.e., 'did not avert' (his gaze). دو دیده 'two eyes' expresses 'both eyes' (= his gaze). از روی شاه 'from the face of the king' uses از to mark separation/source (he did not take his eyes away from the king's face).
segments.s441-g23: چو is the conjunction 'when.' برخاست is the past 'rose' and takes آواز کوس 'the sound/cry of the drum' as its theme (آواز followed by the instrument noun کوس, without an overt ezāfe in poetic style). از دو روی means 'from two sides' (both flanks), so the whole phrase means 'when the drum-signal rose from both sides.'
segments.s441-g24: بی‌آرام شد is a copular change-of-state: بی‌آرام 'restless/disturbed' + شد 'became.' مردم جنگجوی is the subject 'the warlike people' or 'warriors' (مردم 'people' modified by the adjective/noun جنگجوی). Word order places the predicate (became restless) before the subject, a common poetic inversion.

sentences.s441-s33: For a year there was mourning in Sistan; all their garments were black and dark-blue.
sentences.s441-s5: When the king of Kabulistan learned of those famed men of Zabulistan.
sentences.s441-s6: He gathered the scattered host; the earth became iron, the sky lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s441-s7: Faramarz received them with his host; light shone from the sun and the moon.
sentences.s441-s8: As the host came into view, the world became loud and belligerent.
sentences.s441-s17: They slew so many from the Indian ranks, and also struck down from among the famed, well‑born men of Sind.
sentences.s441-s18: The battlefield's earth turned to mire, and the armies of Hind and Sind were scattered.
sentences.s441-s19: They took heart away from the frontier and from their houses (i.e. they fled), leaving behind women and small children.
sentences.s441-s20: They hurled the Kabuli chief's body, spattered with blood, into the elephant's chest/howdah.
sentences.s441-s25: He placed forty of his kinsmen on the fire; then from that place he went toward Shaghad.
sentences.s441-s26: Like a mountain he kindled a fire; he burned Shaghad, the plane tree, and the earth.
sentences.s441-s27: When the army moved toward Zabulistan, it drew all the soil toward (the) hands.
sentences.s441-s28: When the days of oppression were shortened, he made a certain elder king in Kabul.
sentences.s441-s29: From that line there remained no one in Kabul who did not read the sword's proclamation to him.
sentences.s441-s30: He came from Kabul, full of grief and smoke; in the clear daylight he was entirely bruised.
sentences.s441-s31: All of Zabulistan roared together and closed in; not one of them had a proper garment on his body.
sentences.s441-s32: They returned before Faramarz; they came torn and with anguish.
sentences.s441-s13: Faramarz, with his meager troops, hurled himself upon that very heart (core).
sentences.s441-s14: From the horsemen's dust the air grew dim; the commander of Kabul was trapped.
sentences.s441-s15: That great host — the brave men of Zabul — was scattered like wolves.
sentences.s441-s16: They set ambushes against them on every side; then the troops charged.
sentences.s441-s21: They brought the army to the hunting-ground, to a place where they had dug a pit.
sentences.s441-s22: He took the hostile one with his hands bound; and from his relatives, likewise, forty idolaters.
sentences.s441-s23: He drew it from the spahbed’s back — ah! — so that bone and sinew came into view.
sentences.s441-s24: They hung him into the well and he fell prostrate; his body full of dust and his mouth full of blood.
sentences.s441-s1: When Farāmarz raised Rostam's lament, he sent the whole army toward the Hamun plain.
sentences.s441-s2: He opened (it) in the house of Piltan; from his father's treasure he equipped the army.
sentences.s441-s3: At dawn a clamor rose from the trumpets, and likewise from the drums, from iron, and from Indian [instruments/wealth].
sentences.s441-s4: An army from Zabul marched to Kabul, and the sun seemed to vanish from the world.
sentences.s441-s9: From the multitude of elephants and the dust of the host, the lion lost his way inside the thicket.
sentences.s441-s10: A wind arose and a dusky cloud of dust; from the sky the earth was not visible at all.
sentences.s441-s11: Farāmarz came before the army; he did not take his two eyes off the face of the king.
sentences.s441-s12: When the sound of the war-drum rose from both sides, the warlike people became restless.
